retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: The sickle cell mutation occurs when negatively charged glutamate is replaced by a neutral valine at the sixth position of the beta-globin chain. The mutation is transmitted via Mendelian genetics and is inherited in an autosomal codominant fashion. [5] A homozygous mutation leads to the severest form of SCD, ie, SCA- also called HBSS disease. The coinheritance of beta-naught thalassemia and sickle cell mutation leads to HBS-Beta-0 disease, which phenotypically behaves like HBSS disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Trait -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell trait is a genetic condition that results when an individual inherits a gene for normal hemoglobin (A) and a gene for sickle hemoglobin (S) that results in the genotype (AS). Conversely, sickle cell disease occurs when an individual inherits 2 abnormal sickle genes (SS). There are different types of sickle cell disease, including hemoglobin SS (HbSS), hemoglobin SC (Hb SC), and hemoglobin S beta-thalassemia. These hemoglobin types cause a vaso-occlusive crisis; however, HbSS is the most common and severe type. HbSC results when an individual inherits HbS from 1 parent and HbC from the other. Hb C is caused by a switch from glutamic acid to lysine. HbS beta-thalassemia is a rare mutation that occurs when an individual inherits beta-thalassemia hemoglobin from 1 parent and HbS from the other. However, a vaso-occlusive crisis does not occur just because sickling hemoglobin is present; vaso-occlusion occurs due to multiple events, including deformed red blood cells, increased red blood cell fragility, and increased cation permeability and stickiness. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Trait -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell trait is caused by abnormal hemoglobin called sickle hemoglobin or Hb S, secondary to a point mutation in the beta-globin chain. This point mutation replaces A with T at codon 6 of the beta hemoglobin chain, causing the switch from glutamic acid to valine amino acid. The valine-type hemoglobin causes red cells to sickle when exposed to a low oxygen threshold. Patients with sickle cell trait inherit HbS from 1 parent and HbA from the other, making them heterozygous.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Disease (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell disease (SCD) is a multisystem disorder and the most common genetic disease in the United States, affecting 1 in 500 African Americans. About 1 in 12 African Americans carry the autosomal recessive mutation, and approximately 300,000 infants are born with sickle cell anemia annually. The understanding of the phenotypic expression of the disease is still limited. However, environmental factors such as cold weather and air quality, infections, fetal hemoglobin level, and other genetic subtypes play a role in the manifestation of the disease. Clinical manifestations are variable and affect multiple systems, and generally cause lower life expectancy. [1] [2] [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Within the umbrella of SCD, many subgroups exist, namely sickle cell anemia (SCA), hemoglobin SC disease (HbSC), and hemoglobin sickle-beta-thalassemia (beta-thalassemia positive or beta-thalassemia negative). Several other minor variants within the group of SCDs also, albeit not as common as the varieties mentioned above. Lastly, it is essential to mention the sickle cell trait (HbAS), which carries a heterozygous mutation and seldom presents clinical signs or symptoms. Sickle cell anemia is the most common form of SCD, with a lifelong affliction of hemolytic anemia requiring blood transfusions, pain crises, and organ damage. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Hemoglobin Synthesis -- Clinical Significance -- Sickle Cell Trait and Disease
retrieved_docs.content: The most common abnormal hemoglobin variant is HbS (sickle cell hemoglobin). HbS results from a substitution of the sixth amino acid in the beta-globin subunits. The genetic mutation results in the replacement of glutamic acid with valine and occurs most frequently in African Americans. Heterozygous individuals have a mutation in only one of the two beta chains, resulting in sickle cell trait. Resistance to falciparum malaria infection and complications are benefits of a sickle cell trait. Homozygous individuals have mutations in both beta chains, resulting in sickle cell disease. When deoxygenated, HbS causes the deformation of erythrocytes from a biconcave disc to a crescent or “sickle” shape. This change in shape causes damage to erythrocyte membranes, premature destruction of erythrocytes, and chronic hemolytic anemia. Sickled erythrocytes can obstruct blood flow and cause tissue hypoxia, which can cause severe ischemic pain or even stroke. These patients also have functional asplenia and are at risk for infections with encapsulated organisms. [6] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Disease (Archived) -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell is caused by a mutation in the hemoglobin beta chain in which glutamic acid is substituted with valine at position six on chromosome 11. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: Most patients with HbSS phenotype do not present with classical 'sickle cell crises' soon after birth. HbF is still present in the blood, helping maintain adequate tissue oxygenation, and it takes around 6-9 months to wean off completely. Not all SCAs have the same phenotype, and multiple phenotypes exist that can either co-exist or present as a spectrum of the disease. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: A heterozygous inheritance leads to HbAS. Patients with HbAS are not considered within the spectrum of SCD as most of them never present with typical symptoms of SCA. They might only be detected during childbirth, blood donation, or screening procedures.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Crisis -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell disease is an autosomal recessive disorder of a gene mutation. On chromosome 11, nucleotide mutation leads to substituting glutamic acid to valine at position 6 on the β-globin subunit. This leads to changes in the physical properties of the globin chain. Many inciting factors lead to this physical property change of red blood cells, including hypoxia, dehydration, exposure to cold or weather changes, stress, and infections. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Hematopoiesis -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia results from a nonconservative missense mutation, which involves a single nucleotide change in the β-globin gene region. [23] This mutation causes RBCs to adopt a "sickle" shape when deoxygenated. These sickled RBCs, which contain hemoglobin S (HbS), can continuously change their form, leading to their premature destruction intravascularly. [24] In their sickled shape, the RBCs tend to entrap themselves in the capillaries, causing vasoocclusion. Excessive vasoocclusion can result in a sickle cell pain crisis.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Hepatopathy -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Heterozygous individuals for the ßs allele carry the sickle cell trait and do not have sickle cell disease. However, individuals who are homozygous for the ßs allele have sickle cell anemia. [2] The HbS gene is found in African countries, India, the Caribbean, and Central and South America. In the United States, 1 in every 360 African American newborns has sickle cell disease. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Several other compound heterozygotes exist where a single copy of the mutated beta-globin gene is coinherited with a single copy of another mutated gene. The second most common variant of SCD is the HbSC disease, where the sickle cell gene is coinherited with a single copy of the mutated hemoglobin C gene. HbC is formed when lysine replaces glutamine at the sixth position on the beta-globin chain. HbSC disease accounts for 30% of patients in the United States.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Trait -- Differential Diagnosis
retrieved_docs.content: Differential diagnoses of sickle cell trait include different types of sickle cell disease, beta-thalassemia major, and beta-thalassemia minor.

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: HbSC disease accounts for 30% of all patients with SCD in the US. As with HbAS, patients with the Hb C trait (heterozygous mutation) also remain asymptomatic for most of their lives. Although considered a milder variant of SCD, HbSC disease may present with severe morbidities. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Sickle Cell Anemia -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell disease (SCD) refers to a group of hemoglobinopathies that include mutations in the gene encoding the beta subunit of hemoglobin. The first description of SCA 'like' disorder was provided by Dr. Africanus Horton in his book The Disease of Tropical Climates and their Treatment (1872). However, it was not until 1910 when Dr. James B Herrick and Dr. Ernest Irons reported noticing 'sickle-shaped' red cells in a dental student (Walter Clement Noel from Grenada). [1] In 1949, independent reports from Dr. James V Neel and Col. E. A. Beet described the patterns of inheritance in patients with SCD. In the same year, Dr. Linus Pauling described the molecular nature of sickle hemoglobin (HbS) in his paper 'Sickle Cell Anemia Hemoglobin.' Ingram Vernon, in 1956, used a fingerprinting technique to describe the replacement of negatively charged glutamine with neutral valine and validated the findings of Linus Pauling. [2]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 4 -- Community 2812
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, which is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hereditary hemoglobin disorder that can precipitate serious respiratory complications in adults. Clinically, داء الكريات المنجلية is associated with respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and adult cardiac arrest, underscoring the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt intervention in patients affected by this disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 4 -- Community 2762
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with قساح.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1802
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this hemoglobin disorder commonly manifests as نقص الأكسجين في الدم, indicating reduced oxygen levels in the blood.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1871
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with قساح. A principal manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (low blood oxygen), which is also mapped to الاِختِنَاق, indicating a link between hypoxemia and asphyxic events. Recognizing that patients with sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) may present with قساح and experience نقص الأكسجين في الدم that can progress to or be interpreted as الاِختِنَاق is important for accurate diagnosis, monitoring, and timely therapeutic intervention.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2605
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية) is clinically associated with thalassemia (الثلاسيميا). Both disorders are hemoglobinopathies that can coexist in the same patient, and the presence of thalassemia may influence the ocular manifestations observed in sickle cell disease. Recognizing this association underscores the importance of comprehensive ophthalmic assessment and coordinated care for individuals affected by either or both conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 10 -- Level 3 -- Community 1774
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease. This condition is clinically associated with قَرحَة (ulcer), reflecting the propensity for vaso‑occlusive events and tissue ischemia that can lead to ulcer formation. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (blood oxygen deficiency or hypoxemia), which results from impaired red‑cell flexibility and consequent reduced oxygen delivery. Recognizing that sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) can present with ulcers and low blood‑oxygen levels is important for comprehensive patient assessment and targeted management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 2 -- Community 898
retrieved_docs.content: patients with thalassemia may develop retinal complications characteristic of sickle‑cell disease. All of the aforementioned anemia subtypes—فقر الدم الوبيل, فقر الدم اللا تنسجي, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد, فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية—as well as التهاب المفاصل (arthritis) and التهاب الشريان التاجي (coronary artery inflammation) are listed as members of اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ), the standardized medical query for immune‑mediated or autoimmune disorders. Although فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية is defined as a non‑immune hemolytic anemia, it is still grouped within the immune‑disorder category for classification and reporting purposes, reflecting a pragmatic rather than a pathophysiological contradiction. Together, these relationships illustrate how various deficiency and hemolytic anemias are organized under a common classification, how specific anemias relate to ocular complications, and how many of these

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 133
retrieved_docs.content: is noted for lacking an autoimmune mechanism despite its inclusion in the broader immune‑disorder framework. Thalassemia is also associated clinically with sickle‑cell retinopathy (اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية). Collectively, these entities illustrate that various deficiency and hemolytic anemias, especially those within the “other deficiency anemia” group, share immunologic associations and frequently intersect with chronic kidney disease and other immune‑mediated conditions, highlighting the need for coordinated hematologic, immunologic, and nephrologic assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2612
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is classified as a hemoglobinopathy. In the context of this disease, respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) is identified as a clinical complication, and the Arabic term الاِختِنَاق (asphyxia) is likewise listed as an allowed qualifier complication. Both respiratory failure and asphyxia are closely linked to نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia), which is a direct manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية. The mapping of الاِختِنَاق to نقص الأكسجين في الدم highlights that low blood‑oxygen levels are a central pathophysiological feature that underlies these serious complications. Recognizing that sickle cell disease can precipitate severe respiratory compromise and hypoxemic states is essential for effective patient monitoring and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 229
retrieved_docs.content: “اضطرابات مناعية/المناعة الذاتية (SMQ).” Specifically, فقر الدم الوبيل, فقر الدم اللا تنسجي, التهاب المفاصل, فقر الدم الانحلالي المكتسب, فقر الدم الانحلالي الناجم عن المناعة الذاتية, فقر الدم الانحلالي من النوع البارد, and فقر دم انحلالي ليس ناجمًا عن المناعة الذاتية are all recorded as members of this SMQ immune‑disorder set, highlighting their immunological or autoimmune components. Clinically, الثلاسيميا is associated with اعتلال الشبكية بالخلايا المنجلية (sickle‑cell retinopathy), suggesting overlapping vascular complications between thalassemia and sickle‑cell‑related retinal disease. In the circulatory domain, “other circulatory disease” is linked to الذئبة الحمراء المجموعية (systemic lupus erythematosus), indicating that lupus can manifest with additional circulatory manifestations. This “other circulatory disease” category is positioned under the parent disorder اضطراب الأنسجة الضامة (connective‑tissue disorder) and includes the SMQ immune‑disorder group as a member, further

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2093
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia, also referred to by its Arabic name داء الكريات المنجلية, is a hematologic disorder that can clinically manifest as adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest. This respiratory complication is classified under the broader category of respiratory disorders (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ), which themselves are subtypes of asphyxia (الاِختِنَاق). The disease’s manifestation of low blood‑oxygen levels (نقص الأكسجين في الدم) reflects the same pathophysiologic process that defines asphyxia. Consequently, sickle cell anemia is linked to hypoxemia, which underlies the development of respiratory failure, situating the condition within a hierarchical framework that progresses from the specific hematologic disease to respiratory disorder and ultimately to the general concept of asphyxia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 4 -- Community 2876
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as the disease entity dاء الكريات المنجلية (sickle cell disease). The condition dاء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with severe adult respiratory complications described as respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult). Both dاء الكريات المنجلية and the respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult) condition are identified as allowed qualifiers for complications (komplikacje), indicating that respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest are recognized complications of sickle cell disease and should be closely monitored in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1518
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, and this disease is clinically associated with adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, and arrest. A key manifestation of داء الكريات المنجلية is نقص الأكسجين في الدم (hypoxemia). The condition الاِختِنَاق (apnea) is also mapped to نقص الأكسجين في الدم, linking apnea to the same hypoxemic state seen in sickle cell disease. Together, these relationships underscore that patients with داء الكريات المنجلية are at risk for severe respiratory complications, including hypoxemia and apnea, and highlight the importance of vigilant respiratory monitoring and oxygen management in their care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2381
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia is classified as داء الكريات المنجلية, the Arabic term for sickle cell disease. داء الكريات المنجلية is clinically associated with severe adult respiratory complications, including respiratory failure, respiratory insufficiency, and arrest. In addition, داء الكريات المنجلية is linked to قساح, a term referring to the vaso‑occlusive crisis that characterizes acute painful episodes in this hemoglobinopathy. These interconnections underscore the need for vigilant respiratory monitoring and prompt management of crises in patients with sickle cell disease.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 555
retrieved_docs.content: Sickle cell anemia (داء الكريات المنجلية) is classified as a distinct hematologic disorder and is clinically associated with adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest, as well as with قساح. It commonly manifests as hypoxemia (نقص الأكسجين في الدم). Adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest (respiratory failure; insufficiency; arrest (adult)) is a subtype of respiratory disorder (اضطراب تَنَفُّسِيّ), which itself is a subtype of asphyxia (الاِختِنَاق). Asphyxia is mapped to hypoxemia, underscoring the central role of reduced blood oxygen levels in these conditions. Radiotherapy (radioterapie) is an allowed qualifier for sickle cell anemia, for adult respiratory failure, insufficiency, or arrest, and for asphyxia, and it can be further qualified by قساح. This interconnected framework highlights how sickle cell disease, severe respiratory complications, and hypoxemia are related, and it delineates the contexts in which radiotherapy may be considered as a therapeutic qualifier.
